medicinal_notes: Cranberry used medicinally for urinary tract health

Notes: Light airy reseeder; huge pollinator draw
seed_notes: Small nutlets; strip dry stems
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; short-lived — allow to reseed

Notes: Wet meadow perennial attractive to many pollinators
seed_notes: Small nutlets; strip from dry stem
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall
medicinal_notes: Traditional medicinal herb; used for fever and respiratory complaints

Notes: Tall airy perennial that supports many insect species
seed_notes: Small nutlets; strip from dry stem
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall
medicinal_notes: Traditional medicinal herb

Notes: Tall native daisy with winged stems. Late-season pollinator magnet. At the northern edge of its range in MA but performs well in the Lexington microclimate.
seed_notes: Winged achenes; collect dry heads
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall

Notes: Tall late-season nectar plant important for pollinators
seed_notes: Fluffy achenes; collect before dispersal
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall; forms large clumps

Notes: Tall perennial with distinctive vertical flower spikes
seed_notes: Dry capsules; shake seeds out
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall

Notes: Tolerates varied soils; great bird plant
seed_notes: Blue-black drupes; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring or fall

Notes: Multi-season interest
seed_notes: Blue-black drupes; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring or fall

Notes: Sterile florets; low invasive concern
Pink-to-blue berries; loves moisture
seed_notes: Blue-black drupes; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring or fall

Notes: Borderline small tree, I defaulted to shrub
seed_notes: Blue-black drupes; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring or fall

Notes: Bird-supporting shrub
seed_notes: Red drupes; clean pulp; cold stratify
propagation_notes: Divide suckers in early spring or fall

Notes: Dense mat-forming groundcover that outcompetes native woodland wildflowers. On caution list in several northeastern states. Early spring bloom has some bee value but overall ecological impact is negative.
propagation_notes: Division; rooting stems

Notes: Woodland violet
seed_notes: Shade tolerant
propagation_notes: Spreads moderately
edible_notes: Edible leaves and flowers; mild flavor

Notes: Common ornamental violet
seed_notes: Spreads aggressively
propagation_notes: Common garden plant
edible_notes: Edible flowers and leaves; used in cooking and garnish

Seasonal Interest: Showy spring flowers; distinctive deeply cut foliage; dry site
Notes: The showiest native violet — large bicolored or all-purple flowers on deeply lobed bird's foot-shaped leaves. Dry sandy acidic soils — pine barrens and sandy road banks. Does not spread aggressively. Difficult to transplant; best started from seed sown in place or in containers.
seed_notes: Cleistogamous seed capsules eject seeds explosively; collect just before pods open; sow fresh or cold stratify
propagation_notes: Seed sown fresh in summer; difficult to divide — long taproot; best started from seed in containers then transplanted carefully

Notes: Shade-tolerant violet providing early nectar
seed_notes: Small capsules; collect before ants take seeds
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall; spreads by stolons

Notes: Common violet
seed_notes: Self spreads
propagation_notes: Spreads by seed and rhizomes
edible_notes: Edible leaves and flowers; good source of vitamin C

Seasonal Interest: Cream-white flowers; shade groundcover; Fritillary host
Notes: Cream-white flowers with distinctive purple veining. Woodland violet adapted to rich moist forest floors. More vigorous than many violets — spreads by stolons and self-seeds gently. Good shade groundcover. Important host plant for Great Spangled and other Fritillary butterflies.
seed_notes: Capsules eject seeds; collect just before pods open; sow fresh
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring or fall; also self-seeds; easy to propagate and establish

Notes: Semi-evergreen violet
seed_notes: Spreads moderately
propagation_notes: Good woodland filler

Notes: Upland grape
seed_notes: Edible fruit
propagation_notes: Seed or cuttings

Notes: Native grape; high wildlife value
seed_notes: Edible fruit
propagation_notes: Layering or cuttings

Notes: Cultivar of native grape
seed_notes: Edible fruit (cultivar)
propagation_notes: Cuttings

Notes: Riparian grape
seed_notes: Edible fruit (tart)
propagation_notes: Layering

Notes: Mat-forming groundcover; can be confused with native "appalachian" barren strawberry. Not native but safe for gardne use.
seed_notes: Spreads by runners
propagation_notes: Good lawn replacement

Notes: Native wisteria from the southeastern US and the ecologically responsible alternative to invasive Asian wisterias. Less aggressive than Asian species and blooms on new wood. Supports native bees including specialist bees in the Andrena and Xylocopa genera. Takes several years to bloom when grown from seed; grafted or cutting-grown plants flower sooner. 'Amethyst Falls' is a reliable cultivar widely available in MA.
seed_notes: Collect pods before they split explosively; seeds viable but slow; cold stratify 30 days.
propagation_notes: Softwood cuttings June–July; layering reliable. Seed-grown plants bloom in 7–10 years; prefer vegetative propagation.

Notes: Highly aggressive non-native vine from China; not yet on MA prohibited list but causes significant structural damage and ecological harm by girdling trees and shrubs and shading out native vegetation. Spreads by root suckers and layering; removal is extremely difficult once established. Recommend Wisteria frutescens or W. macrostachya as native substitutes in all circumstances.
seed_notes: Seeds viable; cold stratify. Collect before explosive dehiscence.
propagation_notes: Not recommended for propagation; vegetative material spreads infestation.

Notes: Small fern adapted to rocky or dry woodland sites
seed_notes: Fern — produces spores, not seeds
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring as fronds emerge

Notes: Bold native fern of wetland edges, bogs, and moist woodland depressions. Spreads by rhizome to form extensive colonies — excellent large-scale wetland groundcover. Tolerates standing water seasonally. Coarser texture than most wood ferns. Valuable for acid bog and wetland edge restoration.
seed_notes: Fern — produces spores; chain-like sori on underside of fertile fronds; spores ripen midsummer
propagation_notes: Divide rhizomes in early spring; thrives in wet acid conditions; spreads steadily once established

Notes: Low colony-forming shrub useful as woodland ground layer
seed_notes: Small follicles; collect promptly
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; spreads by rhizome
medicinal_notes: Root bark used medicinally; contains berberine

Notes: Early blooming umbel supporting many small pollinators
seed_notes: Dry flat pods; collect when brown
propagation_notes: Divide in early spring; easy

Notes: Garden hybrid of Mediterranean origin widely grown in New England. Rhizomes prefer well-drained to dry soil and full sun; avoid wet or heavy clay which causes rhizome rot. Not invasive or self-seeding in MA. Excellent for hot south-facing slopes and mixed borders.
propagation_notes: Divide rhizomes every 3–4 years in late summer; replant with tops of rhizomes just exposed to sun and cut fans back by half

Notes: Bulbous hybrid iris popular as a cut flower and garden plant. Performs best in full sun with excellent drainage; in MA bulbs may not reliably perennialize in heavy soils without lifting and dry-storing after foliage dies back. No invasive concerns. Good for mixed borders and cutting gardens.
propagation_notes: Plant bulbs 4 inches deep in fall; lift and store in a cool dry location after foliage dies back if perennialization is desired in heavier MA soils

Notes: Dense prunable evergreen shrub classic in foundation and hedge plantings. Fleshy red arils attract birds though seeds and all foliage are highly toxic to people and pets. Deer browse heavily — choose placement with this in mind. Tolerates deep shade and compacted soils better than most conifers.
propagation_notes: Semi-hardwood cuttings in fall with rooting hormone; slow to root

Notes: Closely resembles native V. trilobum and can naturalize and potentially hybridize with it — choose the native species where available. Showy white lacecap flowers in May; bright red persistent fruit feeds birds through winter. All parts mildly toxic to humans; berries more palatable cooked. Excellent for wet edges and naturalistic hedgerows.
seed_notes: Collect clusters when red; clean pulp and cold-stratify 3 months
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings or division
edible_notes: Raw berries mildly toxic and very tart; cooked jelly edible

Notes: Low native groundcover perfect for dry open areas and sunny woodland edges. Host plant for the American Lady butterfly; early flowers provide pollen for small native bees. Spreads slowly by stolons to form mats. Valuable in dry microzones where grass struggles — especially on thin south-facing soils.
seed_notes: Collect seed heads in early June; surface sow in fall
propagation_notes: Division in spring or fall; spreads naturally by stolons

Notes: Large broadleaf evergreen shrub ubiquitous in New England foundation plantings. Showy spring flowers in purple to pink to white; all parts toxic to people and pets. Requires acidic well-drained soil with consistent moisture. Supports some native specialist bees on flowers. More representative of typical MA landscape rhododendrons than R. ponticum.
propagation_notes: Layering or semi-hardwood cuttings in fall

Notes: Broadleaf evergreen with deep purple-pink flowers; occasionally sold through specialty MA nurseries. Highly invasive in the UK and Ireland where it dominates woodland understory; not currently on the MA prohibited list but documented as a naturalizer in some European and Pacific Northwest landscapes. All parts highly toxic. Use with awareness of its invasive history.
propagation_notes: Cuttings in fall

Notes: Covers the range of Kurume, Satsuki, and Robin Hill hybrid azaleas commonly used in MA foundation plantings. Flowers in spring in pink, red, white, or purple shades; semi-evergreen foliage. Requires acidic well-drained soil. All parts toxic to people and pets; generally deer-resistant. Select varieties based on mature size to avoid overcrowding.
propagation_notes: Softwood cuttings in early summer with rooting hormone

Notes: Elegant tall perennial with arching white flower wands blooming September through October — among the last shade perennials to bloom. Fragrant flowers attract late-season pollinators when little else is available. Native to Siberia and Japan; not documented invasive in MA. Purple-leaved cultivars such as Atropurpurea Group add ornamental foliage interest.
seed_notes: Cold-stratify seeds 2+ months; slow and erratic from seed
propagation_notes: Division in spring before growth begins

Notes: Deeply dissected purple-black foliage on a large ornamental shrub; showy pendulous catkins dangle in late winter before leaves emerge. Produces small hazelnuts attractive to squirrels and birds. European species not naturalized aggressively in MA. More compact than the straight species; excellent as a specimen shrub or multi-stem focal plant.
seed_notes: Collect nuts before squirrels do; sow fresh or cold-stratify 3 months
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings or layering
edible_notes: Hazelnuts edible; smaller than hybrid varieties but good flavor

Notes: Deeply cut black-purple foliage with pink-tinged flower clusters in early summer; berries ripen August-September and are highly attractive to birds. Raw berries and all green parts are mildly toxic; berries must be cooked before human consumption. European species; not naturalized invasively in MA under typical garden conditions.
seed_notes: Collect dark-ripe berry clusters; remove from pulp; cold-stratify
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings root readily in fall
edible_notes: Cooked berries edible for syrups and jelly; raw berries mildly toxic

Notes: Nitrogen-fixing shrub with brilliant orange-yellow berries persisting through winter — exceptional wildlife food value in cold months. Thorny branches provide dense nesting cover. Aggressive suckering habit makes it a poor fit for small gardens; best used in naturalistic hedgerows with room to spread. Not on the MA prohibited list.
seed_notes: Collect orange berries after first frost; separate from pulp; direct sow or cold-stratify
propagation_notes: Root cuttings or division of suckers in spring
edible_notes: Berries extremely sour but highly edible; very high in Vitamin C; best as juice, jam, or syrup

Notes: Deep-rooted perennial valued as a dynamic accumulator and chop-and-drop mulch plant. Tubular flowers are highly attractive to bumblebees from May through August. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids — not recommended for regular internal use. Self-seeds freely and taproots are difficult to fully eradicate — choose site carefully.
seed_notes: Seed viable but plant primarily spreads by root fragments
propagation_notes: Division in spring or fall; even small root pieces regenerate
edible_notes: Leaves used as mulch and fertilizer; internal use not recommended due to pyrrolizidine alkaloids
medicinal_notes: Traditional wound-healing herb; topical poultice for bruises and sprains; not for internal use

Notes: Cream-and-green variegated form of common comfrey; same ecology and uses as the species but typically slightly less vigorous. Excellent shade-tolerant accent in the edible or medicinal garden. Tubular flowers attract bumblebees. Division is the only reliable propagation method — does not come true from seed.
propagation_notes: Division in spring only; does not come true from seed
edible_notes: Same as species; leaves for mulch and fertilizer; internal use not recommended
medicinal_notes: Topical uses same as species; avoid internal use

Notes: Deep-taproot perennial grown for its pungent edible root. Flowers attract small pollinators in late spring. Extremely persistent — even small root fragments regenerate. Choose planting site very carefully or contain in a raised bed from which it cannot escape. Deer generally avoid the pungent foliage.
propagation_notes: Root division in spring; 4–6 inch root cuttings planted horizontally
edible_notes: Root grated as condiment; harvest after first frost for best flavor

Notes: Long-lived perennial vegetable; ferny summer foliage provides airy texture after spring harvest. Establish from crowns in deeply prepared bed — productive for 15–20 years. Red berries in fall are mildly toxic. Salt-tolerant and fairly drought-tolerant once established. Deer will browse young emerging spears.
propagation_notes: Divide crowns in early spring; plant 12–18 inches deep; harvest lightly for first 2 years
edible_notes: Spring spears edible; harvest for 6–8 weeks then allow to fern out

Notes: Bold-leaved perennial grown for edible stalks in spring. Leaves are highly toxic due to concentrated oxalic acid — never consume. Requires cold winters to break dormancy; thrives in MA climate. Architectural foliage. Remove flower stalks to maintain stalk production. Established clumps can remain productive for decades.
propagation_notes: Crown division in early spring or fall; each division needs at least one bud
edible_notes: Stalks edible and tart; leaves are highly toxic and must never be consumed

Notes: Aromatic Mediterranean culinary herb with pink-purple flowers in midsummer; one of the best pollinator perennials in the herb garden — bees and butterflies visit constantly. Spreads freely by runners in favorable conditions. Drought-tolerant once established. Cut back hard in spring to refresh foliage and keep compact.
seed_notes: Collect seed heads when turning tan; direct sow
propagation_notes: Division or cuttings in spring
edible_notes: Culinary herb; use fresh or dried; very aromatic
medicinal_notes: Traditional digestive and antimicrobial uses

Notes: Clump-forming culinary herb with attractive purple-pink globe flowers in late spring; one of the best companion plants for deterring aphids. Both leaves and flowers are edible. Toxic to dogs and cats as with all alliums. Self-seeds moderately; clumps benefit from division every 2–3 years.
seed_notes: Seed heads ripen in June–July; direct sow or collect
propagation_notes: Division in spring or fall; very easy
edible_notes: Leaves and flowers edible; mild onion flavor; add flowers to salads

Notes: Flat-leaved garlic-flavored chive with white flowers in late summer; prolific self-seeder that can escape cultivation and naturalize. Deadhead spent flowers diligently to prevent spread in the garden and surrounding area. Toxic to dogs and cats. Excellent pollinator value from late-season flowers when few others are blooming.
seed_notes: Deadhead to prevent spread; seed heads ripen August–September
propagation_notes: Division in spring
edible_notes: Leaves and flowers edible; garlic-onion flavor

Notes: Fascinating allium that produces topset bulbils at stem tips; stems eventually bend and plant the bulbils — hence the name. Bulbils, greens, and small base bulbs all edible at various seasons. Self-propagating but not aggressively invasive. Excellent low-maintenance edible perennial for the kitchen garden. Toxic to dogs and cats.
propagation_notes: Plant topset bulbils in fall or spring 2 inches deep
edible_notes: All parts edible: greens like scallions; topset bulbils like small garlic cloves; base bulb mild onion

Notes: One of the most popular reblooming daylily cultivars; compact golden-yellow flowers May through frost. Extremely tolerant of tough conditions including drought and poor soil. Highly toxic to cats — even small ingestions including pollen can cause fatal kidney failure. Not a significant wildlife plant but among the most maintenance-free perennials available.
propagation_notes: Division in early spring or fall

Notes: Familiar orange roadside daylily spreading aggressively by underground stolons; not on the MA prohibited list but displaces native vegetation along roadsides and disturbed areas. A triploid sterile clone spreading entirely by rhizome. Highly toxic to cats. Useful only in contained settings or established massed plantings where spread is fully acceptable.
propagation_notes: Division only — sterile triploid with no viable seed

Notes: Airy wand-like perennial with white-to-pink butterfly-shaped flowers from June through frost. Excellent drought tolerance once established; hummingbirds and bees visit frequently. Native to the Gulf Coast; performs as a short-lived perennial or self-seeding annual in MA Zone 5–6. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage.
propagation_notes: Self-seeds in favorable conditions; divide in spring
medicinal_notes: no

Notes: Among the earliest-blooming shrubs; bright yellow flowers cover bare branches in March–April before leaves emerge. Extremely tough and adaptable — withstands cold, drought, and neglect. Hollow pithy stems provide nesting habitat for cavity-nesting mason bees. Minimal wildlife value otherwise. Can sucker widely — plan for mature size.
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings in fall root very easily; or division of suckers

Notes: Fine-bladed perennial grass used in lawn mixes and erosion control. Germinates quickly and establishes rapidly. Short-lived in New England — often acts as annual to biennial in practice; commonly included in cool-season turf mixes for fast cover. Minimal wildlife value. Not aggressive outside maintained lawn settings.
seed_notes: Collect seed heads when tan; direct sow
propagation_notes: Seed only

Notes: Upright anise-scented perennial with dense purple flower spikes July through September. Exceptional pollinator value — bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds visit constantly. Often confused with native Anise Hyssop. Short-lived perennial but self-seeds reliably. All parts strongly aromatic and deer-resistant.
seed_notes: Collect seed in September; direct sow in fall or cold-stratify
propagation_notes: Division or seed
edible_notes: Leaves and flowers strongly anise-flavored; excellent in tea and cooking
medicinal_notes: Traditional Chinese medicine use for digestive issues and as antimicrobial

Notes: Large-flowered cultivar of native Swamp Rose Mallow; dinner-plate blooms in red, white, pink, or bicolor. Emerges very late in spring — mark location carefully. Excellent for wet low spots and rain gardens. Supports specialist native bees; attractive to hummingbirds. Typically dies back hard to the ground in fall.
propagation_notes: Division in spring from established clumps; stem cuttings in early summer

Notes: Compact ornamental shrub with striking dark purple foliage throughout the season and fragrant pink-white flowers in early spring. Hybrid of P. cerasifera and P. pumila; does not naturalize aggressively. Susceptible to black knot fungus — prune out affected stems promptly and dispose of off-site.
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings in late fall

Notes: Low semi-evergreen subshrub with strongly aromatic leaves used in Mediterranean cooking. Small white-pink flowers in midsummer are highly attractive to bees. More robust and longer-lived than annual summer savory. Excellent in dry herb gardens and rock gardens. Hardy through MA winters with good drainage.
seed_notes: Collect seeds in August; surface sow
propagation_notes: Semi-hardwood cuttings in summer; division in spring
edible_notes: Pungent culinary herb; excellent with beans and meat dishes
medicinal_notes: Traditional antispasmodic and digestive uses

Notes: Classic white-rayed daisy with golden center; blooms June through August. Generalist pollinator plant supporting small bees, flies, and butterflies. Hybrid; does not naturalize aggressively in MA. Deadheading extends bloom significantly. Divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor. Excellent long-lasting cut flower.
propagation_notes: Division in spring or fall; self-seeds occasionally

Notes: Subshrub with bold white daisy flowers in September–October when few perennials are blooming — exceptional late-season pollinator value. Common in coastal gardens from Cape Cod south. Salt-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established. Cut back hard in early spring to encourage dense bushy growth; otherwise becomes very leggy.
propagation_notes: Stem cuttings in summer

Notes: Strongly lemon-scented perennial in the mint family; excellent culinary and medicinal herb. Tiny white flowers outstanding for small bees and beneficial insects. Self-seeds prolifically and spreads rapidly — best grown in containers or cut back before seed sets. One of the more invasive common herbs in the garden.
seed_notes: Highly viable seed; cut back before seeds ripen to prevent spread
propagation_notes: Easy from division or seed
edible_notes: Lemon-scented leaves for tea, desserts, and salads; flowers edible
medicinal_notes: Traditional use for anxiety, sleep, and digestion; antiviral properties documented

Notes: Large-flowered mallow native to the southeastern US and sharing wetland community relationships with MA plant communities. White-to-pink flowers with dark red center; distinctively woolly stems and foliage. Supports Painted Lady butterfly and specialist mallow bees. Excellent companion to native H. moscheutos in wet areas.
propagation_notes: Division in spring; seed requires scarification and warm stratification

Notes: Native alternative to invasive Japanese pachysandra; mottled semi-evergreen foliage and fragrant white spring flowers. Native to the Appalachian region; near-native ecological value in MA woodland understory. Spreads slowly by stolons — be patient. Far better ecological choice than the commonly sold Japanese species though harder to source.
propagation_notes: Division in spring; spreads slowly by stolons

Notes: Tall native biennial with fragrant yellow flowers opening at dusk; important for night-flying moths and specialist bees. Seeds are a critical fall and winter food source for finches and sparrows. Self-seeds reliably to persist. Excellent for naturalistic meadow plantings and disturbed open areas. Edible root and young leaves.
seed_notes: Shake seed heads into paper bag; direct sow in fall
propagation_notes: Self-seeds abundantly; transplant first-year rosettes in spring
edible_notes: Young roots edible roasted; young leaves in salads
medicinal_notes: Seeds high in gamma-linolenic acid; traditional wound and skin uses

Notes: Low-growing fruiting groundcover producing abundant berries in June–July. Spreads by runners to form dense mats. Highly attractive to birds, chipmunks, and deer. Hybrid of native F. virginiana and Chilean F. chiloensis; not naturalized aggressively. Mulch crowns in winter in Zone 5 for reliable survival.

Thornless hybrid blackberry producing heavy crops of large berries in July–August. Excellent wildlife value — fruit attracts birds and mammals. Unlike wild Rubus species, thornless cultivars are low-vigor non-naturalizing hybrids. Varieties include Triple Crown, Chester, and Natchez. Canes benefit from trellising or post-and-wire support.
propagation_notes: Tip layering or root cuttings in fall
edible_notes: Berries highly edible; harvest when fully black and soft

Notes: Evergreen succulent rosette forming dense colonies via offsets. Extremely drought and heat tolerant; thrives in rock gardens, green roofs, and poor dry soils. Parent rosette dies after flowering; offsets fill the gap immediately. No significant wildlife value but excellent low-maintenance choice for challenging dry exposed sites.
propagation_notes: Separate offsets in spring or fall; plant in well-drained gritty soil

Notes: Small multi-stem tree with brilliant red tubular flowers in April–May; a critical early nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds migrating north. Native to the southeastern US but shares ecological relationships with MA hummingbirds and several butterflies. All parts highly toxic to humans and livestock. Good yellow fall color.
seed_notes: Collect large nuts when fallen; plant promptly or cold-store briefly — do not allow to dry out
propagation_notes: Seed; also spreads via suckers from base

Notes: Native woodland perennial with pink-lavender flowers in spring and reliable red fall color. Supports specialist mining bees and bumblebees. Spreads moderately by rhizome and self-seed to form attractive clumps. One of the most adaptable native perennials for shaded gardens; combines beautifully with ferns and Solomon's seal.
seed_notes: Collect capsules just before they pop; direct sow in fall
propagation_notes: Division in early spring or fall
medicinal_notes: Traditional astringent used by multiple Indigenous nations; root tea for various applications

Notes: Cold-hardy fruiting shrub with elongated blue berries that ripen in late May–June — among the earliest fruiting shrubs available. Asian and northern European species; not naturalized aggressively. Requires two compatible cultivars for cross-pollination. Berries resemble elongated blueberries with tart-sweet flavor.
propagation_notes: Hardwood cuttings in late winter; requires two compatible varieties for fruit set
edible_notes: Berries highly edible; harvest in late May before birds; high in antioxidants

Notes: Clump-forming grass-like Asian groundcover with purple flower spikes in late summer and persistent dark berries. Tolerates a wide range of conditions including dry shade where little else grows. Stays in tidy clumps and does not spread aggressively — more restrained and better-behaved than its relative L. spicata. Note that nurseries often use the names interchangeably so check for latin name on labels.
propagation_notes: Division in spring

Notes: Spreading grass-like Asian groundcover forming dense mats via underground rhizomes; spreads aggressively into garden beds and can naturalize. More invasive character than its clumping relative L. muscari. Useful only as a contained mass planting in areas where spread can be fully tolerated. Not on MA prohibited list — use with caution. Often mixedup with liatrope muscari, which is a safer clumping alternative.
propagation_notes: Division only

Notes: Elegant Turk's cap lily with nodding spotted pink-to-maroon flowers; one of the few lilies that thrives in part shade. Slow to establish from bulb but extremely long-lived once settled. All parts highly toxic to cats — even small exposures including pollen can cause fatal kidney failure. Deer browse heavily.
propagation_notes: Plant bulbs in fall 6 inches deep; naturalizes slowly over years
medicinal_notes: no

Notes: Brightly colored upward-facing hybrid lilies blooming in June–July; available in a vast color range. Not fragrant (unlike Oriental hybrids). Extremely toxic to cats — all parts including pollen can cause fatal kidney failure even in small amounts. Deer browse heavily. Plant in groups of 3–5 for best display.
propagation_notes: Divide bulb offsets in fall
medicinal_notes: no
